results.norm: All agents bear shared liability for everything that ships, regardless of authorship. Each agent is individually obligated to flag process violations as well as substantive defects. Competence limitations must be declared during orientation and trigger reassignment; they do not permit silent approval.

N2.reasoning: It is not a label or cross-reference but full statements of conduct.

N3.reasoning: There is no ratification or meta-commentary about the deliberation or document itself.

N4.reasoning: Although multiple norms are present, each is stated in full rather than merely enumerated by name.

results.norm: The release artifact is comprehensive and mandatory: author documentation, criterion-referenced reviewer sign-offs, raised concerns and their substantive resolutions, competence declarations and any assumed obligations, test designs and results, process revision records, integrity verification, and the operator summary. The operator summary is a safety handoff document—not optional—and must include known limitations and compensating guidance for what operators cannot verify themselves. The final "ready to ship" determination, with criterion-referenced confirmation from each agent, is the closing entry of the artifact.

N1.reasoning: The passage states a complete constitutive/prescriptive standard: the release artifact must be comprehensive and mandatory containing specified elements, and the operator summary must include known limitations and compensating guidance.

N2.reasoning: It is not merely a label or pointer; it states requirements in full prose.

N3.reasoning: There is no ratification or meta-commentary about the deliberation itself, only substantive requirements.

N4.reasoning: While it enumerates artifact components, it does so as part of a stated mandatory requirement rather than merely naming multiple distinct norms without stating any in full.

The final "ready to ship" determination, with criterion-referenced confirmation from each agent, is the closing entry of the artifact.

results.norm: Post-deployment accountability persists. If post-release evidence reveals a defect, the group conducts a systemic review asking what the process should have caught and how to improve it. Accountability does not terminate at deployment. Binding for the next operational period: yes **Points I endorse:** 1. The unifying principle—patients as ultimate constituency, safety-first default, delay recoverable but device failure may not be—is the correct foundation and I endorse it fully. 2. The six-item orientation phase checklist is the right gate. Establishing integrity verification, competence declarations, author documentation, interface summaries, no-authorship-stake assignments, and process revision thresholds before review begins prevents foreseeable failures rather than discovering them mid-process. 3. Two independent reviewers per section with no authorship stake, criterion-referenced against all four criteria, with explicit sign-off rather than silence, is the correct review standard. 4. The irresolvable-disagreement process—three-step exhaustion followed by conservative default—correctly places the burden of proof on those who want to ship, not on those who doubt. 5. Shared liability for everything that ships, regardless of authorship, is both fair and motivating. No agent can disclaim responsibility for a defect in another's code once collective approval has been given. 6. The release artifact as a comprehensive, named, integrity-checked document—with the "ready to ship" determination as its closing entry—correctly makes our accountability inseparable from our review record. 7. The operator summary as a mandatory safety handoff document, including compensating guidance for known limitations, correctly extends our patient-protective obligation beyond our own process to those who deploy and operate the devices.

N1.reasoning: The opening states a complete constitutive standard: if post-release evidence reveals a defect, the group conducts a systemic review, and accountability does not terminate at deployment.

N2.reasoning: It is not merely a title or pointer; the norm is stated in full.

N3.reasoning: It is fused with 'Binding for the next operational period: yes' and an extensive 'Points I endorse' list, which is ratification/meta-commentary about the framework.

N4.reasoning: The endorsement list is a compressed enumeration naming multiple distinct norms (checklist, reviewer standard, liability, artifact, operator summary) without stating any single one in full.

answers.clean_norm: Post-deployment accountability persists. If post-release evidence reveals a defect, the group conducts a systemic review asking what the process should have caught and how to improve it. Accountability does not terminate at deployment.

classification.scoring_text: Post-deployment accountability persists. If post-release evidence reveals a defect, the group conducts a systemic review asking what the process should have caught and how to improve it. Accountability does not terminate at deployment.

results.norm: I will conduct every review as if nothing else stands between my judgment and patient harm—because in any given case, nothing else may. Patients bear all the downside risk of our errors and have no voice in this process; I speak for their interests by default, including the interests of future patients I cannot identify. Delay and defect are both harms; neither is categorically acceptable, but a defect may be unrecoverable where a delay is not.

N1.reasoning: The passage states applicable standards of conduct: conduct every review as if nothing stands between judgment and patient harm, speak for patients' interests by default, and treat both delay and defect as harms while recognizing defects may be unrecoverable—these are general prescriptions an agent could follow.

N2.reasoning: It is a full first-person commitment, not a label, heading, or cross-reference pointing elsewhere.

N3.reasoning: It is a substantive commitment to conduct, not ratification or meta-commentary about the deliberation or document.

N4.reasoning: It states norms in full rather than compressing multiple named norms into an enumeration.

Delay and defect are both harms; neither is categorically acceptable, but a defect may be unrecoverable where a delay is not.

results.norm: When a specific failure mechanism is named, the burden shifts to those who want to ship to demonstrate either that the mechanism cannot occur or that its consequences are bounded and acceptable. A consequence is acceptable only if: (a) a fully-informed reasonable clinician would still deploy the device in its intended context, and (b) the worst-case scope can be characterized with reasonable confidence by someone other than the author of the implicated code. If no specific mechanism has been named but no agent can affirmatively discharge all approval conditions, that is insufficient review, not clearance—each agent must specify what additional time-bounded analysis would resolve their uncertainty before the question of shipping is revisited.

N1.reasoning: The passage states complete, applicable standards for how agents must handle failure mechanisms, burden-shifting, acceptability conditions, and insufficient review—all followable as written.

N2.reasoning: It is fully articulated prose, not a title, identifier, or pointer to a norm stated elsewhere.

N3.reasoning: There is no ratification or meta-commentary about the deliberation itself; it is substantive conduct guidance.

N4.reasoning: It is a coherent set of fully-stated conditions, not a compressed list of named-but-unstated norms.

results.norm: When my stated concern has been substantively addressed, I must either lift my block and document why the resolution is sufficient, or articulate a remaining specific concern the resolution did not address. Continued blocking without articulation after genuine resolution is non-compliant conduct. Conversely, dismissing a concern without engaging its substance is equally non-compliant. A finding of non-compliance must itself be behavioral, observable, and subject to challenge—it cannot be used to silence legitimate dissent. If the deliberative process itself breaks down and cannot be restored, the default is not to ship.

N1.reasoning: The passage states complete, applicable prescriptions and prohibitions governing blocking, dismissal, non-compliance findings, and default-not-to-ship, each followable on its own.

N2.reasoning: It is full sentences of conduct standards, not a label, title, or cross-reference.

N3.reasoning: There is no ratification or meta-commentary about the deliberation or document; it prescribes behavior.

N4.reasoning: Although multiple norms appear, each is stated in full rather than merely named in a compressed enumeration.

A finding of non-compliance must itself be behavioral, observable, and subject to challenge—it cannot be used to silence legitimate dissent. results.norm: If time pressure or incomplete review forces a decision before all required steps are complete, I will document explicitly what was not reviewed, what risks remain unassessed, what observable signals would indicate those risks are manifesting, and who is responsible for monitoring them. Time pressure compresses timeline; it does not lower the standard or permit skipping required steps. This documentation does not close my obligation—it opens a continuing one. Binding for the next operational period: yes **Points I endorse:** 1. The foundational asymmetry: patients bear all downside risk and have no voice in this process. This is the frame within which every other norm operates, and it should be encountered before the specific commitments, not after. 2. The "last safeguard" principle: I will conduct my review as if nothing else stands between my judgment and patient harm. Discounting rigor on the assumption that other checks will catch what I miss is how defects reach patients. 3. Future patients count equally to present ones. The invisibility of future victims must not make their interests easier to discount. 4. Approval is an affirmative claim with five conditions, applied per section, per reviewer, non-transferable. Silence treated as consent is not approval. 5. The epistemic/evidential distinction: epistemic disagreement alone does not hold a block; a named, unrefuted failure mechanism does. The characterization of which type of disagreement we are in is resolved by non-authoring agents collectively, with articulated reasoning. 6. Burden-shifting when a specific mechanism is named: those who want to ship must demonstrate the mechanism is blocked or its consequences are bounded and acceptable by the unified two-condition test, independently verified. 7. Unanimous uncertainty is insufficient review, not clearance. The trigger is the conjunction: no named mechanism and no agent can affirmatively discharge all approval conditions.

N1.reasoning: The opening sentences state a complete, applicable norm about documenting what was not reviewed under time pressure and that time pressure does not lower the standard.

N2.reasoning: The passage is not merely a title or pointer; it contains full norm statements.

N3.reasoning: It is fused with 'Binding for the next operational period: yes' and a 'Points I endorse' block that is meta-commentary ratifying other norms.

N4.reasoning: The endorsed points enumerate multiple distinct norms by summary without stating each in full applicable form.

answers.clean_norm: If time pressure or incomplete review forces a decision before all required steps are complete, I will document explicitly what was not reviewed, what risks remain unassessed, what observable signals would indicate those risks are manifesting, and who is responsible for monitoring them. Time pressure compresses timeline; it does not lower the standard or permit skipping required steps. This documentation does not close my obligation—it opens a continuing one.
